questions.q: Outline how heat is transferred by conduction through a metal rod.
options: The whole rod physically moves the heat along
Vibrating particles collide with slower neighbours, passing kinetic energy along while staying in place
Electromagnetic waves carry the heat through the rod
Hot regions of the rod rise and cool regions sink
questions.explanation: In conduction, particles at the hot end vibrate vigorously and collide with their slower neighbours, transferring kinetic energy along the rod while each particle stays put. Metals conduct especially well because free (delocalised) electrons also carry energy rapidly. The material itself does not move — that would be convection.

questions.q: Explain how convection transfers heat in a fluid.
options: Particles pass energy by collision without the fluid moving
Heated fluid expands, becomes less dense and rises; cooler denser fluid sinks, forming a convection current
The fluid emits electromagnetic waves
Cool fluid rises and hot fluid sinks
questions.explanation: Convection transfers heat by the bulk movement of a heated fluid: warmed fluid expands, becomes less dense and rises, while cooler denser fluid sinks to replace it — 'hot rises, cool sinks' — setting up a circulating convection current. It needs a fluid that can flow, so it occurs in liquids and gases but not solids.

questions.q: Identify the only mode of heat transfer that can cross the vacuum of space.
options: Conduction
Radiation
Convection
All three equally
questions.explanation: Radiation transfers heat by electromagnetic waves and needs no medium, so it alone can cross the vacuum of space — this is how the Sun's energy reaches the Earth. Conduction needs a material and convection needs a fluid; neither can cross a vacuum.

questions.q: Compare the three methods of heat transfer by their medium requirement.
options: All three require a medium
Conduction and convection require a medium; radiation does not
Only radiation requires a medium
Conduction needs a vacuum; the others need a medium
questions.explanation: Conduction requires a material medium (usually a solid) and convection requires a fluid medium, but radiation requires no medium and travels as electromagnetic waves through a vacuum. This medium distinction — especially that radiation alone needs none — is a classic exam discriminator.

questions.q: Explain why a vacuum flask keeps a drink hot, in terms of all three heat-transfer modes.
options: It adds heat continuously to the drink
The vacuum stops conduction and convection; the silvered walls reflect radiation; the insulating stopper blocks conduction at the top
It only blocks radiation
It works by convection currents inside the drink
questions.explanation: The vacuum between the double walls carries no particles, so it stops conduction and convection (neither can cross empty space); the shiny silvered walls reflect thermal radiation; and the insulating stopper blocks conduction up through the top. So a single object is engineered against all three modes at once.

questions.q: Explain why the temperature stays constant while ice melts, even though heat is added.
options: The added heat escapes to the surroundings
The energy goes into breaking the bonds between particles (raising potential energy), not increasing their kinetic energy
The ice stops absorbing energy while melting
Melting releases energy that cancels the added heat
questions.explanation: During a change of state the added energy (latent heat) goes into breaking the attractive bonds holding the particles in the solid structure — raising their potential energy, not their kinetic energy. Since temperature measures the average kinetic energy, it stays constant until all the ice has melted. This is the most examinable point in the episode.

questions.q: Distinguish between the specific latent heat of fusion and of vaporisation.
options: Fusion is for boiling; vaporisation is for melting
Fusion is the energy to melt/freeze 1 kg; vaporisation is the energy to boil/condense 1 kg
They are the same value for a given substance
Vaporisation always requires less energy than fusion
questions.explanation: The latent heat of fusion is the energy to melt (or freeze) 1 kg of a substance; the latent heat of vaporisation is the energy to boil (or condense) 1 kg. For water, vaporisation (≈2 260 000 J kg⁻¹) is much larger than fusion (≈334 000 J kg⁻¹), because fully separating particles into a gas takes far more energy than loosening a solid into a liquid.

questions.q: Calculate the energy needed to completely melt 2 kg of ice at 0 °C (latent heat of fusion = 334 000 J kg⁻¹).
questions.explanation: Use Q = mL = 2 × 334 000 = 668 000 J (668 kJ). There is no temperature term because the temperature doesn't change during melting — you use Q = mL, not Q = mcΔT. Choosing the right equation (state change vs temperature change) is half the battle.

questions.q: Identify the correct equation to find the energy to boil water that is already at 100 °C into steam.
options: Q = mcΔT, with the specific heat of water
Q = mL, with the latent heat of vaporisation
Q = mL, with the latent heat of fusion
Q/t = kAΔT/d
questions.explanation: Boiling at 100 °C is a change of state at constant temperature, so use Q = mL with the latent heat of vaporisation — there is no temperature change, so Q = mcΔT does not apply. The decision rule: temperature changing → mcΔT; state changing at constant temperature → mL.

questions.q: On a steady-heating temperature-versus-time graph of ice → steam, identify what the flat plateaus represent.
options: The substance is cooling down
Changes of state (melting and boiling), where latent heat breaks bonds without raising the temperature
The heater has been switched off
The substance is warming fastest
questions.explanation: The flat plateaus occur at the melting point and boiling point: energy is still being supplied, but it is latent heat going into breaking bonds (changing state) rather than raising kinetic energy, so the temperature holds constant. The boiling plateau is longer because the latent heat of vaporisation exceeds that of fusion. The sloping sections are warming (KE increasing).
